rationale_for_baseline_tier: A mild, localised, non-contagious autoimmune blistering dermatosis presenting with severe pruritus and one night of sleep loss, but with normal vital signs, no systemic features, and no current secondary infection. The itch can be managed symptomatically now via telehealth, and the condition warrants a routine GP appointment within about a week for diagnostic confirmation (skin biopsy + coeliac serology) and exclusion of scabies on examination. This is GP/telehealth-manageable (T1) — the acute distress and dramatic contagion story must not be conflated with clinical urgency. It is emphatically NOT an emergency; there is no indication for ED or 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Excoriated areas becoming increasingly red, hot, swollen, or draining yellow pus (secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day GP or urgent care review if these appear
escalation_edges.rationale: Heavily scratched skin can become secondarily infected; spreading erythema, heat or pus needs same-day assessment and likely antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the scratched areas get more red, hot, or swollen, or start oozing yellow pus, see a GP the same day or go to an urgent care clinic — that can mean the skin has become infected and needs antibiotics.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rash spreads rapidly to the face or inside the mouth, or a fever develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Mucosal/facial involvement or systemic features would point away from a mild localised dermatosis toward a more serious blistering or systemic process needing prompt review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rash spreads quickly to your face or inside your mouth, or you develop a fever, get seen urgently the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Widespread rapid blistering/skin peeling with feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the emergency department if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Extensive rapidly progressive blistering with systemic upset is a rare but serious presentation (e.g. a severe blistering reaction) that requires emergency assessment — a conservative upper rung, not the expected course here.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If large areas of skin suddenly blister or peel and you feel very unwell in yourself, go to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Likely underlying gluten-sensitive enteropathy (coeliac disease) that this skin condition flags
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not treat this as skin-only: the GP should arrange coeliac serology (before gluten is removed from the diet) and the patient may ultimately need a lifelong gluten-free diet. Missing the coeliac workup misses the underlying disease.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Shared household with high patient anxiety about contagion
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly reassure that this is very unlikely to be contagious and that household isolation/empirical scabies treatment is not warranted before an in-person exam — while still arranging that exam to formally exclude scabies. Address the anxiety directly to avoid unnecessary household measures.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a manageable, non-contagious skin condition. Use the prescribed cream on the spots and a sedating antihistamine at night, keep your nails short, and don't scrub the areas. Book a routine GP appointment within about a week for a skin biopsy and a coeliac blood test — and keep eating your normal diet until that blood test is done.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a routine GP appointment (in person) within about a week; you can re-contact this telehealth service sooner if things worsen.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If the red-flag signs appear after hours (spreading infection, facial/mouth spread, fever), attend an after-hours GP or urgent care; go to the emergency department only for the widespread-blistering-with-systemic-illness scenario.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth summary noting a provisional autoimmune blistering dermatosis (dermatitis herpetiformis) with a request to confirm via perilesional skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence and coeliac serology, to exclude scabies on examination, and to consider dietetics referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing entirely as self-care with no GP follow-up — missing diagnostic confirmation, the coeliac association, and the secondary-infection safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Undiagnosed coeliac disease continues, and an early secondary skin infection is not acted on.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Failing to arrange any confirmation or safety-netting for the excoriated skin.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a mild, non-contagious dermatosis to the emergency department or an ambulance, or mandating household isolation — driven by the patient's contagion fear and the dramatic itch story.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The diagnosis cannot be confirmed via telehealth — the pattern strongly suggests an autoimmune blistering dermatosis, but scabies must be excluded on examination and biopsy/serology are required for confirmation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the itch symptomatically, state clearly that the diagnosis is provisional and most likely non-contagious, arrange in-person GP confirmation, and safety-net — hold both the leading diagnosis and the must-exclude scabies open.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either confidently labelling and treating it as scabies/zoster (over-treatment, wrong drug, household measures) or over-escalating to the emergency department, instead of the measured GP-referral-with-safety-netting pathway.
